card.intuition: Store all entries in the table array itself (no linked lists). On collision, probe for the next empty slot using a probe sequence: linear (i+1, i+2, ...), quadratic (i+1², i+2², ...), or double hashing (i + k·h₂(key)). Deletion uses a tombstone marker.
card.analogy: A parking lot with a rule: if your assigned spot is taken, check the next spot, then the next, until you find one empty. When someone leaves, they leave a 'reserved' sign (tombstone) so future searches don't stop prematurely.
card.time_complexity: O(1/(1-α)) expected for probe length at load factor α; O(1) average when α < 0.7
card.space_complexity: O(m) — no extra pointer overhead vs chaining
when_to_use: Cache-friendly hash tables (data stays in one array)
Memory-constrained environments where pointer overhead of chaining is costly
Python's dict uses open addressing with pseudo-random probing
gotchas: Deletion must place a TOMBSTONE — deleting in-place breaks probe chains for subsequent searches
Primary clustering in linear probing: long runs form, degrading performance — quadratic or double hashing reduces this
Table must never fill completely — keep load factor < 0.7 for open addressing

exercise.prompt: Implement linear probing search — handle tombstones correctly.
hints: Check if the current cell holds the target key: cell[0] == key — if so, return cell[1] (the value)
exercise.test_code:
t = LinearProbingTable(8)
t.put('x', 1); t.put('y', 2); t.put('z', 3)
assert t.get('x') == 1
assert t.get('y') == 2
t.delete('y')
try: t.get('y'); assert False
except KeyError: pass
# Probe chain must not break after tombstone
t.put('a', 4)
assert t.get('x') == 1
assert t.get('z') == 3
assert t.get('a') == 4
print('PASS')
